ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for almond oil as a food substance in the current release; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of almond oil is approximately 1 tablespoon (14 g), used as a finishing oil in salad dressings, drizzled over cooked vegetables, or incorporated into baked goods.

nutrient_source.title: Oil, almond — USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 171412 (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry 'Oil, almond' (FDC ID 171412) is an exact match for refined/pressed almond oil. Core macros and fatty acid profile sourced from this entry. Singapore HPB database values for energy (884 kcal) and fat (100 g) are consistent with USDA data and are retained as corroborating evidence.

gi_source.title: N/A — Glycemic index not applicable for pure fat foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Almond oil is a pure fat (100 g fat per 100 g, 0 g carbohydrate). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI value exists or is meaningful for pure oils. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Zero sodium; MUFA-dominant fat profile supports endothelial function
hypertension.guidance: Almond oil contains zero sodium and is rich in oleic acid (MUFA ~70%), which supports vascular endothelial function consistent with DASH diet principles; use 1 tablespoon (14 g) as a finishing oil or in dressings rather than for high-heat cooking to preserve MUFA integrity.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; MUFA content associated with improved insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Almond oil has zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, producing no glycaemic impact; its high MUFA content (oleic acid ~70%) is associated with improved insulin sensitivity per ADA dietary fat recommendations, but caloric density (884 kcal/100 g) requires portion control to avoid excess energy intake that can worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High MUFA (69.9 g) and PUFA (17.4 g) with low saturated fat (8.2 g); rich in vitamin E (39.2 mg)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Almond oil's favourable fatty acid profile — predominantly oleic acid (MUFA) and linoleic acid (PUFA) with only 8.2 g saturated fat per 100 g — supports LDL reduction and cardiovascular health; its high vitamin E content (39.2 mg/100 g, ~261% DV) provides antioxidant protection against LDL oxidation, consistent with AHA dietary fat guidance.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: MUFA/PUFA dominant with high vitamin E antioxidant content; qualifies for MUFA/PUFA clinical carve-out
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Almond oil qualifies for the MUFA/PUFA clinical carve-out as a cold-pressed plant oil with MUFA (~70%) and PUFA (~21%) dominance and high vitamin E (39.2 mg/100 g); use cold-pressed/virgin almond oil as a finishing oil or in dressings and avoid high-heat frying to prevent oxidation of MUFA into pro-inflammatory compounds.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Almond oil is a pale yellow, clear edible oil extracted from the kernels of sweet almonds (Prunus dulcis) by cold-pressing or expeller-pressing, followed by optional refining. It has a mild, delicate nutty flavour and a smooth, light texture that makes it well suited for use as a finishing oil, in salad dressings, light sautéing, and baking. Nutritionally, almond oil is a pure fat (100 g fat per 100 g) providing 884 kcal per 100 g, with zero protein, zero carbohydrate, and zero sugar. Its fatty acid profile is dominated by monounsaturated fat (oleic acid, ~69.9 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fat (linoleic acid, ~17.4 g/100 g), with a relatively low saturated fat content of 8.2 g/100 g and zero trans fat. It is exceptionally rich in vitamin E (alpha-tocopherol, ~39.2 mg/100 g), a potent lipid-soluble antioxidant that provides approximately 261% of the daily value per 100 g. Almond oil contains zero sodium, zero cholesterol, and negligible amounts of other micronutrients as expected for a refined plant oil. Clinically, its MUFA-dominant profile supports LDL cholesterol reduction, endothelial function, and insulin sensitivity, making it a heart-healthy and diabetes-friendly cooking fat when used in moderation. Its high vitamin E content provides antioxidant protection relevant to cardiovascular disease prevention and inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. Cold-pressed or virgin almond oil retains maximum vitamin E and polyphenol content and is preferred for nutritional benefit over refined variants. Almond oil is a tree-nut-derived product and is a major allergen for individuals with almond or tree-nut allergies. It is suitable for vegan, vegetarian, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and ketogenic diets.
common_user_queries: Is almond oil good for cooking?
What are the health benefits of almond oil?
Is almond oil suitable for diabetics?
How much fat is in almond oil?
What is the smoke point of almond oil?
Can I use almond oil for salad dressing?
meal_context: finishing oil for vegetables and pasta
drizzling over grilled fish or chicken

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy 884 kcal, fat 100 g, protein 0 g, carbohydrate 0 g) are confirmed by both the Singapore HPB database (input source, confidence 0.95) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 171412, which is an exact match for almond oil. Fatty acid breakdown and vitamin E values are sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. All values are internally consistent and cross-validated across two approved sources.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is naturally zero as almond oil is a pure extracted fat with all protein removed during pressing and refining — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171412. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero for the same reason — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171412. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as fibre is absent in refined plant oils — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171412. sugar_g is naturally zero — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171412. sodium_mg is naturally zero for a pure plant oil — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171412. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171412. Glycemic index is not applicable as almond oil contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per the zero-carbohydrate GI rule. Negative sentinel values (-1) documentation: vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, and selenium_mcg are all set to -1 because USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 171412 ('Oil, almond') does not report these micronutrients for almond oil; these B-vitamins and selenium are not expected to be present in meaningful quantities in a refined plant oil, and no approved source (HPB Singapore, USDA SR Legacy) provides values for these fields for this food. Saturated fat 8.2 g/100 g, MUFA 69.9 g/100 g, PUFA 17.4 g/100 g, and vitamin E 39.2 mg/100 g sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171412. Almond oil is a major tree-nut allergen. Cold-pressed/virgin almond oil retains higher vitamin E and polyphenol content than refined almond oil. The food_subgroup has been updated from 'Dressings' to 'Edible plant oils' to better reflect the product category. Ontology: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301502 and FoodEx2 code A00LK are cited as defensible anchors; reviewers should verify these codes against the current FoodOn and FoodEx2 release before production import.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) as almond oil contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g — satisfies the critical q4 rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source documents the rationale referencing the University of Sydney GI Database framework. This satisfies the q4 not-applicable pathway.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301502 (almond oil) and FoodEx2 code A00LK (Almond oil) cited as defensible anchors with exact match type. SNOMED CT set to null with honest gap note. No codes invented.

It covers what the food is, how it is prepared (cold-pressing, expeller-pressing, refining), taste and texture (mild nutty flavour, smooth light texture), nutritional profile (fatty acid breakdown, vitamin E), clinical relevance (MUFA/insulin sensitivity, LDL reduction, vitamin E antioxidant), cultural and dietary context (vegan, keto, allergen), and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (concern_level low, key_factor sodium/MUFA, specific clinical guidance referencing DASH diet), Type 2 Diabetes (concern_level low, key_factor zero carbohydrate/MUFA/insulin sensitivity, specific clinical guidance with ADA reference), Cardiovascular Disease (concern_level low, key_factor MUFA/PUFA/vitamin E, specific clinical guidance with AHA reference). human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly. Rheumatoid arthritis included as additional condition.

Iteration 3 fix — Q4 glycemic completeness: glycemic_index confirmed as null (not 0 and not a string) to satisfy the numeric type requirement for the not-applicable pathway; gi_category remains 'not_applicable'; gi_source remains documented.

Iteration 3 fix — Q6 LLM-ready semantics: Expanded natural_language_description to include explicit preparation method detail (cold-pressing, expeller-pressing, refining), enhanced sensory description, explicit vitamin E DV percentage, explicit dietary suitability statements (vegan, keto, allergen), and clinical context for all three required health conditions.
Preserved all correct sections from iteration 2 draft without modification.
